entry.title: How to remove batch management from product master in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Dear all,

my users made a mistake and created batches for several products, now there is stock, material movements, purchase orders and production orders for these materials and batches become relevant without need.

That is why we want to delete the batch information again.

The batch management setting can not be reversed anymore for the affected products, but creating new products is no option due to released and approved drawings with these product numbers.

In S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition there is no SE16N that could help us with this. What is the way in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to reverse this setting ?

Thank you.

Best Regards,

Dirk

entry.title: Re: Book Depreciation Plan report SAP S/4HANA Public Clou...
entry.content:
Good day @JAS3311 

Thank you for your question and the details provided.

In Public Cloud, it is recommended to use a clean core approach for missing standard point. This involves Released CDS views (VDM) à Custom CDS View à Custom Analytical Query and Services à Export.

See the following references for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3e5fcf2c768746049b5627bd5a42f720/3e4e64781f00498fb2bab41b90aa228c.html?locale=en-US

This Blog provides some good insights: Custom Analytical Queries - SAP CLOUD ERP Public Edition ( SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud )

Here is a quick guide:

Step 1, In your tenant you can search for released asset views. An example is I_FixedAsset, open the view and check if it contains the fields you need.

Step 2 is to build the data source using Custom CDs Views, create a view based on the released asset you chose in step 1, join or combine the needed entities and publish it.

Step 3 is to create the report with Custom Analytical Queries and Services, create a new query with your published Custom CDS View, add the required columns and filters and save it. You may also expose it as a tile if you wish to or export to Excel.

When you build the Custom CDS View and query, you will typically map:

  • Asset value and capitalisation date fields
  • Time dependent asset data (or usage related dates)
  • Depreciation terms
  • Asset values (NBV)
  • Depreciation terms or planned values (this depends on your configuration)
  • Planned depreciation values per fiscal year
  • Depreciation key and method attributes
  • Depending on whether your client uses revaluation or subsequent capitalisation; may require additional value fields
  • Retirement date where it is maintained
  • Depreciation term changes (this is time dependent or change documents)

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: S/4HANA Public Cloud Adobe Forms: How to Control Line Item Layout Using a Header Flag?
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am working on an SAP Adobe Form in S/4HANA Public Cloud. A custom header-level flag field is sent from ABAP and correctly bound to a header field, and the value prints correctly in the PDF output. This flag is used in JavaScript (calculate event) on a repeating line-item subform to control line-item layout behaviour such as visibility or page breaks, based on the current line-item index. The script is placed on a subform.

The logic works correctly in Adobe LiveCycle Designer (ADLC) preview, but after uploading the form and executing it in SAP runtime via transaction code, the conditions do not work. The flag value is available and prints correctly, no JavaScript errors occur, but the line-item layout changes are ignored.

What is the correct SAP-supported way to control line-item layout based on a header-level flag in Adobe Forms in S/4HANA Public Cloud?

 

Thank you.

entry.title: Delta Load from One Datasphere Space to Another (Data Lake Type) – Data Not Overwriting Properly
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am facing an issue with delta loads across SAP Datasphere spaces and would appreciate your guidance.

Scenario Overview

Step 1: BW Bridge Space → Space A

  • We are performing delta data loads from a view in BW Bridge space to a local table in Datasphere Space A using a Transformation Flow.

  • The BW Bridge view is shared with Space A.

  • The local table in Space A is delta-enabled.

  • Load type used: Initial and Delta.

✅ Result:

  • Delta loads are running successfully.

  • Data in Space A matches the data in BW Bridge space

 

Step 2: Space A → Space B (Data Lake Type)

  • The delta-enabled local table in Space A is shared with Space B.

  • Space B is of type Data Lake.

  • A Transformation Flow is created in Space B to load delta data from the shared table (Space A) into a delta-enabled local table in Space B.

  • Load type used: Initial and Delta.

⚠️ Issue:

  • Delta loads are running without errors.

  • The number of delta records loaded from:

    • BW Bridge → Space A

    • Space A → Space B
      are the same.

  • However, data in Space B is not matching Space A.

  • Records are not overwriting properly in Space B.

  • Data is not in sync.

 

Troubleshooting Already Attempted

  • Executed Merge after data load in Space B.

  • Performed table optimization steps in Space B.

  • Issue still persists.

 

Question

Am I doing anything wrong while implementing delta loads from one Datasphere space to another, especially when the target space is of type Data Lake?

Is there any specific consideration for:

  • Delta handling behavior in Data Lake spaces?

  • Primary key / semantic key handling?

  • Overwrite vs append behavior?

  • Merge requirements in Data Lake tables?

  • Delta propagation limitations across spaces?

Any guidance or best practices for this scenario would be greatly appreciated

entry.title: HOW TO FETCH DATA FOR THE LATEST RECORD FROM SAP TABLE USING CDS VIEW
entry.content:
Introduction: Fetching latest record from sap table could be tricky and might end up with the incorrect record and you will see why it is so and how to solve it in this blog. Below is a way to fetch data for the latest record from table EKES by looking up the created-on-date (ERDAT) and time (EZEIT) field of the table for a set of key fields and join this to primary tables like EKKO and EKPO.

  • I have the table EKES in sap and I need to fetch the fields EINDT and MENGE for the latest record for every line of purchasing document.
  • Create a CDS view and define it to query the table EKES.
  • Select ebeln and ebelp as the key fields.
  • Concatenate erdat and ezeit and get the max value of this string grouped by ebeln and ebelp.
define view entity ZCDS_TEST as select from EKES 
{ 
key ebeln, 
key ebelp, 
max(concat(coalesce(erdat,'00000000'), coalesce(ezeit,'000000'))) as max_date } 
group by ebeln, ebelp
  • This will get the latest record.

  • Coalesce function should be used here to make sure that if any field is empty then the length is maintained to 8+6 = 14 digits, so that we do not get the wrong result.
  • Now we want to fetch the EINDT and MENGE value for this record.
  • If we directly fetch these fields in the view, then it will not return correct data as we will have to add them in the group by clause.

  • Below will be the result if we group by ebeln, ebelp, eindt and menge.

  • Instead, we can concatenate these required fields in the max_date field of the view to avoid the fields in group by clause
define view entity ZCDS_TEST as select from EKES 
{ key ebeln, 
key ebelp, 
max(concat(coalesce(erdat,'00000000'), 
concat(coalesce(ezeit,'000000'), 
concat(coalesce(eindt, '00000000'), 
coalesce(menge, 0.000) as abap.char(16))))) as max_date 
} group by ebeln, ebelp
  • The marking in green is the EINDT value and the one in yellow is for MENGE.

  • In the main cds view, join this view with EKKO and EKPO. Get the substring of max_date from position 15 till the next 8 digits. This will be the EINDT value. For MENGE value, get the substring of max_date from the position 23 till the next 16 characters, as menge is 16 digits.
define view entity ZCDS_TEST_JOIN as select from EKKO as a 
left outer join ekpo as b on a.ebeln = b.ebeln 
inner join zcds_test as c on b.ebeln = c.ebeln 
and b.ebelp = c.ebelp 
{ 
key ebeln, 
key ebelp, 
a.ernam, 
b.aedat, 
case when coalesce(c.max_date, '') = '' or substring(c.max_date, 15, 8) like '%00000000' then '00000000' else cast(substring(c.max_date, 15, 8)
 as abap.dats) end as eindt, 
case when coalesce(c.max_date, '') = '' or substring(c.max_date, 15, 8) like '%00000000' 
then '0.000' 
else cast(substring(c.max_date, 23, 16) as abap.dats) 
end as menge 
}
  • You can see the eindt and Menge in the below for key combination of ebeln and ebelp.

Conclusion:

  • We do not have to join the table multiple times for fetching the max date and then for fetching max time for the max date.
  • All logic fits in a single view with a single join on the required tables.
  • Simplifies the query.
  • Helps with performance.

entry.title: Thirty Tools Later
entry.content:
Introduction

I’m an SAP functional consultant by role, but I’ve always had the soul of a developer. This is the story of how that instinct — along with a lot of late nights, AI assistance, and a fair share of dead ends — led me to build something I never thought I could.


Where It All Started

I started building ABAP FS v2 (we call it ABAP Copilot internally) sometime around May 2025. Can’t even remember the exact date anymore. Probably should have written it down.
I realized our developers were not entirely happy with Eclipse ADT and I wanted to build something better (jury is still out on that) but I had no idea where to even begin. Then a friend of mine - Ketan Shenoy introduced me to Marcello Urbani’s ABAP Remote Filesystem extension, and something clicked.

One Tool Became Thirty
It started as a single Language Model Tool - object search (took me a while and a lot of research to learn what LM Tools API is). It let Copilot find ABAP objects in SAP directly from the chat. That’s it.
Then I kept getting ideas. One tool became two - then ten - then thirty. All using VS Code’s Language Model Tools API, talking to SAP via ADT.
The whole thing lived in one massive 6000-lines file for a while - classic rapid prototyping. No architecture, no plan. Just kept adding without even thinking about maintenance. I just wanted it to “work”.


The Dark Nights
Then I realized several features that were available in Eclipse ADT (at least the ones people actually used and wanted in VS Code) were missing - embedded GUI, ADT feeds, etc., and I spent a lot of nights adding them one by one. Dark nights indeed.
I also tried a few things that didn’t work out. At one point I tried embedding custom ABAP objects (including code) into a local ChromaDB and querying it with natural language. My laptop (like most) could only run the basic all-MiniLM-L2-v2 embedding model which has a 8K context limit (learnt it the hard way). ABAP objects are generally much bigger than that. Didn’t work. Scrapped it. Not every idea makes it, and that’s fine (I guess?)


The MCP Problem

When I decided to add MCP support, I assumed I’d have to extract the core logic of each tool into separate files and then build two parallel paths — one for the Language Model Tools, and another for MCP. That meant touching every single tool - tools that were already “working”. Forget implementing MCP - even the thought of refactoring the 6000-line monolith was exhausting.
Then I thought - what if I could just wrap the existing Language Model Tools as MCP tools, without duplicating anything? I didn’t even think it was technically possible. Threw it at Copilot. Turns out it was. One path, two interfaces. I still had to refactor, but the scope was nothing like what I originally imagined. If you think my V2 PR was too big and complicated, you should have seen the pre-refactored version!


What Came Together
After months of iteration, here’s what ABAP Copilot became:
• Code search with regex, across the entire SAP system
• Read, edit, create ABAP objects without leaving VS Code
• SQL runner, Mermaid diagrams for code flow
• Dump analysis, transport comparison, cross-system comparison
• Embedded SAP GUI via WebView — still not sure how many people actually use this one
• ABAP Cleaner with one click formatting
• MCP support so non-Copilot users could connect too
• Subagents, heartbeats, and the orchestrator


Conclusion - The Merge, and What It Means

Finally got the code merged into Marcello Urbani’s ABAP Remote Filesystem. It’s on the VS Code Marketplace now. Thanks again Marcello - for ABAP FS and for accepting my mess of a PR!!
Something that started as a personal experiment is out there, being used (hopefully). Not bad for a functional consultant, I guess. Lots of late nights. Lots of AI-assisted coding sessions. Lots of learnings — about tool calling, about what models are actually good (and bad) at, about when to scrap an idea and when to push through.


Now I spend my time staring at the install count on the VS Code Marketplace and the issues page on the repo.
Still evolving.

entry.title: Your Guide to SAP Customer Experience (CX) Trainings — “SAP Classes”
entry.content:
If you’re building skills across SAP Customer Experience, you’ll notice the training portfolio is organized around the CX suite: Sales & Service Cloud, Commerce Cloud, Incentive Management (Commissions), and Customer Data (CDP/CDC). These offerings fall under SAP Learning Class / instructor-led training (ILT)—live, expert-led courses that combine explanation with hands-on exercises and guided practice.

SAP Sales & Service Cloud

C4H410 — SAP Sales Cloud

In this course you learn SAP Sales Cloud sales business processes end-to-end—covering lead, opportunity, quotation, sales contracts, order and activity management, plus visit planning, forecasting, pricing, add-ons, and integration scenarios. 
Course page: C4H410 — SAP Sales Cloud

C4H440 — SAP Cloud for Customer Administration

A deep dive into admin and implementation setup for SAP Cloud for Customer—spanning business configuration, org structures, master data setup, user/role management, data migration, workflow/approvals, personalization/extensibility, analytics, and mobile features. 
Course page: C4H440 — SAP Cloud for Customer Administration

C4H470 — SAP Sales Cloud Version 2

An instructor-led, hands-on exploration of Sales Cloud v2 aimed at business users and admins—covering lead & opportunity management, quotations and orders, tasking and digital selling workspace, visit planning, and managerial capabilities like pipeline/forecasting tools. The description also highlights AI-era capabilities (e.g., ML/GenAI and lead intelligence concepts) and collaboration configuration (including Microsoft Teams integration).
Course page: C4H470 — SAP Sales Cloud Version 2

C4H510 — SAP Service Cloud

This implementation-focused course teaches configuration of Service Cloud processes—covering service levels (SLAs), categories/work distribution, knowledge base, registered products/installed base, warranties, maintenance plans, time recording, contracts, templates/reporting, ticket hierarchy, and multiple integration scenarios (ERP, FSM, and Commerce Cloud).
Course page: C4H510 — SAP Service Cloud

SAP Commerce Cloud

C4H320 — SAP Commerce Cloud Business User Training

A functional “all-round view” of Commerce Cloud—mixing trainer demos with hands-on exercises across product & web content management (Backoffice/SmartEdit), composable storefront CMS concepts, search, pricing, promotions, order management, customer support, B2B commerce, integrations, and foundations (architecture/security/automation/ImpEx/workflows).
Course page: C4H320 — Commerce Cloud Business User Training

C4H340 — SAP Commerce Cloud Developer Part 1

Introduces core Commerce Cloud development concepts—what’s out-of-the-box vs. custom—plus installation/configuration, extensions, type system & modeling, ImpEx, FlexibleSearch, services/facades, WCMS basics, security, and an introduction to composable storefront used as the standard storefront through the training.
Course page: C4H340 — Commerce Cloud Developer Part 1

C4H341 — SAP Commerce Cloud Developer Part 2

A continuation of Part 1 that expands into advanced, commonly-needed capabilities: Backoffice customization, validation frameworks, personalization, search & navigation (including adaptive search), promotions/coupons, OCC web services, CronJobs, event system, process engine, and order-related features.
Course page: C4H341 — Commerce Cloud Developer Part 2

C4H365 — SAP Commerce Cloud Backoffice Framework Developer Training

A specialized course on the Backoffice Framework—teaching how to build and customize a Backoffice cockpit using widgets, editors, and actions; how components communicate; how to orchestrate layouts/behaviors and test widgets; and how to approach common backoffice customization tasks.
Course page: C4H365 — Backoffice Framework Developer Training

C4H370 — SAP Commerce Cloud Composable Storefront Developer Training

Focused on the Angular-based composable storefront for Commerce Cloud, which communicates with Commerce Cloud via the Commerce REST API. It covers storefront concepts and how to extend/customize—setup, outlets, global configuration, CMS components, styling, routing/SEO and other internals—with hands-on exercises throughout.
Course page: C4H370 — Composable Storefront Developer Training

SAP Customer Data Platform (CDP)

C4H63 — SAP Customer Data Platform Implementation Training

Covers the main concepts and implementation of key CDP scenarios, using hands-on exercises for schema customization (profile/activity), data ingestion via source apps, indicators & segments, downstreaming to outbound apps, and personalization campaigns using audiences, CX flows and journeys—plus B2B capabilities via groups and relationships and CDP APIs.
Course page: C4H63 — Customer Data Platform Implementation Training

SAP Customer Data Cloud (CDC)

C4H62 — SAP Customer Data Cloud Implementation

An implementation + developer-leaning course on CDC core concepts and key scenarios, including data schema, customer identity (screensets/Web SDK), consent, CDC APIs/SDKs, and single sign-on. It also covers B2B CIAM hands-on activities such as organization management, policies/authorization, and related configuration.
Course page: C4H62 — Customer Data Cloud Implementation

Sales Performance Management

HR894 — SAP SuccessFactors Incentive Management Project Team Orientation (fka SAP Commissions)

A stakeholder-focused orientation that provides a high-level understanding of SAP SuccessFactors Incentive Management concepts and capabilities. It explains core functionality and features so organizations can work effectively with consultants to plan and implement configuration—covering areas such as setup (security/preferences/calendars/global values), supporting data, compensation plans, calculation, and payee communications. 
Course page: HR894 — SAP SuccessFactors Incentive Management Project Team Orientation (SAP Commissions)

Quick note on scheduling/availability

Course schedules and availability can change by region or lifecycle status, so always use the course pages to confirm what’s currently offered.

Where to find the latest CX learning offerings

For the latest SAP Customer Experience learning offerings—including updates across commerce, customer data, marketing, sales, and services—visit the SAP Learning Customer Experience page: https://learning.sap.com/products/customer-experience.

Whether you’re strengthening your functional foundation, going deeper on administration and implementation, or leveling up your technical and integration skills, these CX trainings are a great way to build practical, job-ready expertise. Bookmark the Customer Experience learning page, pick the course that matches your role, and keep learning in small, consistent steps—your future projects (and customers) will thank you.

Happy learning—and see you in class!

entry.title: Re: Accounting Principles, Ledgers, and Parallel Accounting
entry.content:
Hi Dave,

I hope your question was clarified at the time and the documentation links helped.

The following SAP Help Portal document, for example, details the assignments of accounting principles to ledgers: Assign Accounting Principles to Ledgers and Company Codes. You make deliberate choices which shouldn't override each other (for example, you either decide for a ledger that you use the corporate accounting principle, or you decide to use local accounting principles. Then you save your settings. But be aware that you can make these changes only as long no postings exist).

Best regards,
Verena

entry.title: Re: Record quantity indicator in center cost
entry.content:
Hello @jantoniomoyano3

Does your record quantities in CO support quantity entry in posting process?

The record quantity on the cost center does not force the system to populate quantity on all FI journal entry line created by logistics postings. For goods receipt, the system typically shows quantity only on the material inventory line because that line is material based. The expense lines are not quantity managed in FI the same way, so quantity may not be displayed if record quantity is active on the cost center and the GL account is marked as quantity relevant.

  • Confirm in your Manage Journal Entries that the line item (EIN or EKG) has Quantity and that layout includes it.
  • Verify the quantity is captured in the material document. Check the item quantity and UoM
  • Then test again with a posting (CO relevant) type that records statistical quantity to a cost center

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: How to remove batch management from product master in...
entry.content: There is no supported way to reverse batch management once it has been activated and used. The safe path is either to continue with batch management (but ignore it operationally), or to introduce new material numbers for future processes. Direct deletion or reversal is not possible in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

entry.title: Re: S/4 HANA Private Cloud Ordering

entry.title: Re: How to remove batch management from product master in...
entry.content:
Dear @Dirk,

Can you reverse batch management in Public Edition?

Yes but only after you remove the dependencies (open docs + stock + existing batches). SAP explicitly states the batch indicator cannot be changed while there are open documents / open stock / existing batches

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3340679

IMPORTANT: Also, if any plant still has stock and the plant-level batch flag is set then the product-level batch flag cannot be removed, so you must ensure the plant-level indicator is not set where stock exists or clear stock + remove plant-level indicator first

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3653689

Best Regards,

Dawid

Dear community,

Customer is required adaptation and enhancement of standard SAP Fiori App for multiple countries/company codes/locations. As the result more than 20 different variants might be needed for the same app. In addition, target users are not capable to use Adapt UI properly as key-users so all changes are done centrally by development team. What is the authorisation process to make sure that each location accesses only their app variant? (We need to block access to standard app and other variants)

 

Thank you,

BR Semyon

entry.title: Re: TRM- Daily Compounding calculation logic
entry.content:
Good day @ktemma 

Thank you for your question and the details provided.

If you keep SOFR + Margin in the same nominal interest condition, the compounding calculation type applies to the full effective rate (reference rate plus spread). There is no standard switch that compounds only the reference rate portion while excluding the margin inside the same condition logic.

See the following references for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/e5ec5859d8e54df98492d80564a734c0/682d3fcfc9ad4b4289bdba925f156bd0.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/e5ec5859d8e54df98492d80564a734c0/d617da531198434de10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/b51655f09e1144828aeafec20470b9c8/f0fafb18962a4337b321b988028e4f8a.html?locale=en-US

As a workaround, you can achieve SOFR compounded, margin not compounded by splitting the pricing into two conditions:

  • SOFR only in a variable interest condition with Compound Interest Calculation (to compound daily)
  • Margin as a separate parallel interest condition that is not capitalised (simple accrual or separate cash flow)

See Creating Accumulating Conditions (Interest Rate Instruments) and Run Accrual/Deferral for more details.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: The Hidden Cost of Custom Code in SAP IS U — And How BTP Reduces It Without a Big Bang
entry.content:
Executive Summary

For years, SAP IS‑U has quietly carried the weight of utility operations. Billing accuracy, regulatory compliance, and meter‑to‑cash stability always came first — and rightly so. But the side effect of that stability is a landscape full of Z‑programs, user exits, and enhancements that were built for good reasons at the time, yet now create friction when utilities try to modernize.

As the industry shifts toward decarbonization, DER growth, and real‑time customer expectations, the question isn’t “Is custom code a problem?” It’s “How do we reduce its impact without breaking the systems that keep the lights on?”

SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) offers a practical, low‑risk way to separate custom logic from the core and prepare for S/4HANA — without a disruptive rewrite or a multi‑year freeze.

1. The Custom Code Reality Inside SAP IS‑U

 Most custom code in IS‑U wasn’t a mistake. It was a necessity.

Utilities added enhancements to:

  • Meet state‑specific regulatory rules
  • Support complex rate structures
  • Integrate with legacy CIS, AMI, and market systems
  • Automate workflows long before APIs or event frameworks existed

Over time, this created:

  • Thousands of Z‑objects
  • Enhancements buried deep inside billing, device management, and FICA
  • Business logic tightly coupled to SAP’s upgrade cycle

What once delivered flexibility now slows down change.

2. The Hidden Costs Most Programs Underestimate

2.1 Upgrade and Migration Friction

Every enhancement inside the core increases:

  • SP and EHP regression testing effort
  • S/4HANA conversion complexity
  • Dependency on niche IS‑U technical expertise

Custom code does not just slow upgrades — it dictates upgrade timing.

2.2 Operational Risk

Many utilities still rely on:

  • Poorly documented enhancements
  • Knowledge concentrated in a few long‑tenured developers
  • Custom logic triggered deep inside critical transactions

This creates silent operational risk — especially during incident response or regulatory audits.

2.3 Innovation Tax

Each new digital initiative must answer an uncomfortable question:

“Do we modify the core again, or build around it?”

When the core is overloaded with custom logic, innovation slows — not because ideas are weak, but because change becomes expensive and risky.

3. Why ‘Rewrite Everything’ Is Not a Strategy

Most utilities cannot absorb:

  • A full custom code rewrite
  • A large‑scale CIS replacement
  • A multi‑year freeze on business change

A big‑bang approach often introduces more risk than it removes.

A more realistic path is selective decoupling — and that’s where BTP fits naturally.

4. How SAP BTP Reduces Custom Code Impact — Pragmatically

BTP gives utilities a way to shift custom logic out of the core while keeping IS‑U stable.

4.1 Keep the Core Clean — for Real

Instead of embedding logic in IS‑U:

  • Business rules move to BTP services
  • Extensions run side‑by‑side, not inside ECC or S/4HANA
  • Core processes remain upgrade‑friendly

4.2 Event‑Driven Extensions

Using events and APIs:

  • IS‑U triggers events (billing completion, device updates, move‑in/out)
  • BTP processes logic asynchronously
  • Failures are isolated, not system‑wide

4.3 Reusable Innovation Layer

Once on BTP, the same extension framework can support:

  • DER orchestration
  • AI‑based billing analytics
  • Customer self‑service enhancements
  • Regulatory reporting automation

This turns custom logic into assets, not liabilities.

5. Reference Architecture (Conceptual)

Below is a simplified conceptual architecture illustrating how SAP IS‑U and BTP coexist.

This model allows utilities to:

  • Retire high‑risk custom code gradually
  • Introduce new capabilities without touching the core
  • Prepare for S/4HANA on their own timeline

6. A Phased, Utility‑Friendly Adoption Model

Phase 1: Observe

  • Identify custom code hotspots
  • Monitor usage, performance, and change frequency

Phase 2: Isolate

  • Move non‑core logic to BTP extensions
  • Replace enhancements with APIs and events

Phase 3: Optimize

  • Retire unused Z‑objects
  • Standardize extension patterns
  • Accelerate future innovation

This approach delivers value within existing budgets and without destabilizing operations. 

7. Final Thought for Utility Leaders

The goal is not to eliminate custom code overnight. The goal is to stop letting yesterday’s decisions dictate tomorrow’s options.

SAP BTP provides a practical, low‑risk path to reduce technical debt, lower long‑term cost, and modernize SAP IS‑U — without a big bang and without compromising the core.

If you are evaluating S/4HANA readiness, IS‑U modernization, or clean‑core strategies, the conversation should start with architecture, not migration dates.

If you are evaluating S/4HANA readiness, IS‑U modernization, or clean‑core strategies, the conversation should start with architecture — not migration dates.

entry.title: Mass Invoice Reversals Without Customer Panic: A Clean Core, BTP Driven Strategy for ModernUtilities
entry.content:
Mass Invoice Reversals Without Customer Panic: A Clean‑Core, BTP‑Driven Strategy for Modern Utilities

Executive Summary

If you’ve spent any time around utility billing operations, you know mass reversals aren’t rare — they’re part of the job. Sometimes it’s a rate issue, sometimes a meter read problem, and sometimes it’s just one of those unexpected data surprises that ripple across months of billing, these reversals often span multiple billing cycles and thousands of customers. While the operational process may take days to complete, the customer impact is immediate: as soon as reversals post, customers see large credits on their online portals. This triggers confusion, social media chatter, call‑center spikes, and reputational risk before corrected bills are ready.

What I want to share here is a practical way utilities can handle this without rewriting IS‑U or scrambling teams at 6 AM. We used SAP BTP to create a small visibility layer that sits between IS‑U and the customer portal — and honestly, it solved a problem that has bothered billing teams for years, event‑driven architecture, and lightweight AI to prevent premature credit visibility without modifying SAP IS‑U or customer‑facing systems. The solution introduces a “Billing Visibility Orchestration Layer” that controls what customers see during mass reversals, ensuring operational accuracy without customer panic.

The Practitioner Reality: When a Simple Reversal Becomes a Public Crisis

Every utility billing team has lived through this scenario:

  • A mass correction requires reversing 8–12 months of invoices.
  • The reversal job runs for hours or days.
  • As soon as reversals post, customers see large credits on the portal.
  • Screenshots appear on social media: “Utility X just gave me $900 credit!”
  • Call centers are overwhelmed.
  • Regulators ask questions.
  • The billing team is still in the middle of the correction.

The root cause is simple: SAP IS‑U posts reversals immediately, and customer portals read them immediately. And here’s the part nobody talks about: IS‑U has not built‑in buffer. The moment a reversal posts, the customer sees it. There’s no ‘hold this until we’re done’ button. That’s where the trouble starts. Utilities cannot legally notify customers in advance, and they cannot delay the operational correction. This creates a structural gap between internal billing processes and external customer visibility.

Why This Happens: A Technical Gap in Traditional IS‑U

SAP IS‑U was designed with a real‑time posting model:

  • Reversal document posts →
  • FICA updates →
  • Portal reads →
  • Customer sees credit

There is no concept of:

  • “Temporary suppression”
  • “Billing in progress”
  • “Visibility hold”
  • “Batch publish”

This is not a failure of IS‑U — it’s simply a design that predates modern digital customer expectations.

The Modern Solution: A Billing Visibility Orchestration Layer on SAP BTP

Instead of modifying IS‑U or customer portals, utilities can introduce a lightweight, clean‑core extension on SAP BTP that controls visibility, not billing.

This layer sits between:

SAP IS‑U → BTP → Customer Portal / CIS / Mobile App

It does not change billing logic. It does not alter FICA. It does not modify the portal. It simply orchestrates when billing information becomes visible.

Key Capabilities

  • Detect mass reversals in real time
  • Flag accounts as “under correction”
  • Temporarily suppress credit visibility
  • Replace credit display with a neutral message
  • Release final billing once corrections are complete

This simple layer kept customers from seeing half‑baked credits and gave the billing team the breathing room they needed to finish the job properly.

Textual Representation

 

How It Works Using BTP Services

1. SAP Event Mesh — Detect Reversals in Real Time

Event Mesh listens for:

  • Billing document reversals
  • FKK document reversals
  • Mass job triggers

When reversal volume exceeds a threshold (e.g., >500 accounts), it triggers the suppression workflow.

2. SAP BTP Workflow — Manage the Correction Window

Workflow orchestrates:

  • Start of reversal window
  • Suppression period
  • Completion of rebilling
  • Release of final invoice

This creates a controlled, auditable process.

3. SAP BTP Business Rules — Define Visibility Logic

Rules can be configured without coding:

  • “If reversal count > X, suppress visibility for Y hours.”
  • “If account is in correction, show neutral message.”
  • “If rebill fails, extend suppression.”

This keeps the solution clean‑core and upgrade‑safe.

4. SAP HANA Cloud — Temporary Data Store

Stores:

  • Accounts under correction
  • Suppression flags
  • Workflow status
  • Rebill completion indicators

No changes to IS‑U tables.

5. SAP BTP AI — Predict and Prevent Customer Impact

AI can:

  • Identify accounts likely to call
  • Detect social media spikes
  • Predict rebill duration
  • Recommend suppression windows
  • Flag anomalies in reversal patterns

This turns a reactive process into a proactive one.

What the Customer Sees Instead

Instead of a large credit, the portal displays:

“Your billing is being updated. Your final balance will be available shortly.”

This avoids:

  • Panic
  • Misinterpretation
  • Social media escalation
  • Call center overload

Clean‑Core Alignment

This approach is fully clean‑core because:

  • No IS‑U modifications
  • No FICA changes
  • No portal rewrites
  • No custom Z‑tables in SAP
  • All logic runs on BTP
  • Fully upgrade‑safe
  • Works with ECC and S/4HANA

This is exactly what SAP means by “extensibility without modification.”

Practitioner Example

In one of the utilities I worked with, we had to reverse nearly 10 months of bills for over 180,000 customers. Within 45 minutes, screenshots of huge credits were all over social media. The call center was drowning. Regulators were pinging leadership. It was chaos — and all because the portal updated too early.

After implementing a BTP‑based visibility layer:

  • Mass reversals ran silently
  • No customer saw temporary credits
  • No social media chatter
  • No call center spike
  • Corrected bills were published cleanly

The billing team completed the same process with zero customer disruption.

Conclusion

Mass invoice reversals will always be part of utility operations. But customer visibility does not need to be immediate or uncontrolled. By introducing a Billing Visibility Orchestration Layer on SAP BTP — powered by Event Mesh, Workflow, Business Rules, HANA Cloud, and AI — utilities can protect customer experience, reduce operational risk, and maintain clean‑core principles.

This is modernization where it matters: not in rewriting billing logic, but in orchestrating how and when customers see the results.

entry.title: Re: lease contract & ROU
entry.content:
Hello @siyu1 ,

A lease contract fulfills the requirement by:

-Using multiple valuation views
-Assigning each valuation view to a different accounting principle
-Linking accounting principles to ledger groups
-Generating ledger-specific accounting documents automatically

This allows:

1. ROU capitalization in Ledger 0L
2. Expense-only treatment in Ledger 2L
3. Separate accounting documents
4. Single operational lease contract

 

Requirement / TopicExplanation / How It Works in SAP
Parallel ledgers activatedEach accounting standard is assigned to a separate ledger (e.g., 0L = Standard A, 2L = Standard B). Parallel accounting must be fully configured in Finance.
Standard A requires ROU capitalizationIn the lease contract, create a valuation view linked to Accounting Principle A (assigned to ledger 0L). System posts ROU Asset + Lease Liability + Depreciation + Interest in this ledger.
Standard B requires expense-only treatmentCreate a second valuation view linked to Accounting Principle B (assigned to ledger 2L). Configure valuation rule as expense-only. System posts periodic lease expense only in this ledger.
Need separate accounting documentsSAP generates ledger-group specific postings. During periodic posting run, separate accounting documents are created automatically per ledger group.
How SAP differentiates postingsAccounting Principle → Ledger Group → Valuation View mapping controls posting logic. In Universal Journal (ACDOCA), field RLDNR (Ledger) separates accounting treatments.
Do we need two lease contracts?No. One lease contract supports multiple valuation views and handles different accounting treatments simultaneously.
Posting processLease periodic posting run reads valuation views and posts ledger-specific documents according to assigned accounting principle.
Configuration required1. Define Accounting Principles 2. Assign Accounting Principles to Ledger Groups 3. Define Valuation Areas in Lease Accounting 4. Assign Valuation Area to Accounting Principle 5. Maintain multiple valuation views in lease contract
Result in Financial StatementsLedger 0L shows capitalization (ROU model). Ledger 2L shows expense-only model. Financial reporting per ledger reflects respective accounting standard.
Relevant SAP Help DocumentationSAP Help Portal topics: • “Parallel Accounting in S/4HANA” • “Multiple Valuation in Lease Accounting” • “Ledger-Specific Postings” • “Parallel Valuation in Asset and Lease Accounting”
Relevant SAP Notes (Check Version Applicability)• SAP Note 2344012 – Parallel Accounting in Lease Accounting • SAP Note 2247020 – Lease Accounting Integration with New Asset Accounting • SAP Note 2591969 – Ledger-Specific Posting Logic • SAP Note 2833413 – Valuation Differences in Parallel Ledgers
Final ConclusionOne lease contract can fulfill different accounting treatments by using multiple valuation views mapped to separate ledger groups. SAP automatically creates separate accounting documents per ledger.

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: What is the SAP field in material master for the conn...
entry.content:
Hello @PlamenAKirilov ,

There is no single MM/SD field exclusively for SAF-T linkage. SAF-T reporting uses standard material master fields (MATNR, MTART, MATKL, etc.) extracted via DRC CDS views. Any additional mapping is handled in Document and Reporting Compliance, not directly in MM or SD customizing.

 

Question / AreaExplanation, SAP Help & Notes
Which SAP module links Material Master to SAF-T?SAF-T reporting is handled via SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC) (formerly ACR). It extracts data from MM/SD/FI tables via CDS views and country-specific content. There is no direct MM or SD “module” that links fields manually; mapping is handled within DRC configuration and delivered content.
Is there a specific MM/SD customizing node for SAF-T field mapping?No standard MM or SD customizing exists to directly map material master fields to SAF-T tables. The linkage is predefined in DRC SAF-T XML structures and CDS extraction logic. Enhancements require CDS extension or BAdI implementation in DRC.
Which material master fields are typically used for SAF-T?Standard fields commonly extracted include: • MATNR (Material Number) • MAKTX (Material Description) • MTART (Material Type) • MEINS (Base Unit of Measure) • TAX classification fields (e.g., TAXM1 in sales views). These are read from MARA, MAKT, MVKE depending on country template.
What SAP field connects material to SAF-T “ProductType” or similar nodes?Usually derived from MTART (Material Type) or product classification fields. Some country SAF-T versions derive product category from material group (MATKL). Exact mapping depends on country localization package delivered in DRC.
How does SAP technically map fields to SAF-T XML?Through CDS Views and XML mapping delivered in DRC content. The XML tags are mapped to CDS fields (which read MM tables). This is not classic field-to-field customizing but data extraction configuration.
If a custom field is needed in SAF-T?Extend CDS View (via Custom Fields & Logic app in S/4HANA) and enhance XML mapping using DRC configuration BAdI.
Where to check extraction logic?Use transaction / app for DRC configuration → Check “Define Data Sources” and CDS views used for SAF-T country version. Technical team can analyze CDS view assigned to SAF-T report.
Relevant SAP Notes (Verify for your country/version)• SAP Note 2733610 – SAF-T Reporting in S/4HANA • SAP Note 2788616 – Document and Reporting Compliance Overview • SAP Note 2248229 – Advanced Compliance Reporting Framework • Country-specific SAF-T notes (search: “SAF-T + Country + S/4HANA”)

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: Correction of Trading Partner in Intercompany Invoice...
entry.content:
Hello @AlvaroFernandezMorales ,

Posted accounting documents are legally protected and technically non-editable.

There is:

  • No table update

  • No OSS repair note

  • No backend correction

  • No mass change app for Trading Partner in P&L

Only reversal + reposting ensures audit compliance.

Action for posting.

 

  • Reverse the original intercompany invoice.

  • Confirm Intercompany indicator is active in document type.

  • Repost the invoice.

  • Validate Trading Partner (VBUND) appears in both:

    • Customer line

    • Revenue line

  • Re-run consolidation validation.

 

Situation / QuestionExplanation, Resolution & SAP Notes
Intercompany indicator was not active during postingTrading Partner (field VBUND) is derived at posting time based on document type and intercompany configuration. Since the indicator was not active, derivation did not occur for the revenue (P&L) line. 📌 SAP Note 2453614 – Trading Partner Derivation in Intercompany Postings
Customer line has Trading Partner but revenue line does notCustomer master contains Trading Partner, so subledger line was populated. However, P&L line requires proper intercompany setup to propagate VBUND. System behavior is standard. 📌 SAP Note 2714344 – Universal Journal (ACDOCA) Field Derivation Logic
Configuration corrected after postingChanges to document type (Intercompany indicator) apply only to future postings. Existing documents are not recalculated automatically. 📌 SAP Note 2344012 – Intercompany Posting Behavior in S/4HANA
Can Trading Partner be updated in posted document?❌ No. In S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, posted accounting documents cannot be edited. Field VBUND in ACDOCA is not changeable after posting. 📌 SAP Note 2831001 – Restrictions on Changing Posted Accounting Documents in S/4HANA Cloud
Can “Post Balance Sheet Reclassification – Post Master Data Changes” fix this?❌ No. This job only applies to balance sheet accounts and does not update P&L accounts or overwrite Trading Partner values. 📌 Refer SAP Help: Post Master Data Changes – Balance Sheet Reclassification
Is there any correction report or technical fix?❌ No table updates, debug corrections, or OSS repair reports are allowed in Public Cloud due to audit protection of financial postings. 📌 SAP Note 2831001 (document immutability in Cloud)
What is the only compliant solution?✅ Reverse the original invoice and repost after correcting configuration. This ensures correct Trading Partner derivation in both customer and revenue lines.
If period is closed and reversal not possiblePost adjusting journal entries to reverse and repost revenue correctly. Original document remains unchanged, but financial impact is corrected.
Impact on ConsolidationMissing Trading Partner affects intercompany elimination in group reporting. Correction via reversal/reposting is required to ensure accurate consolidation. 📌 SAP Help: Intercompany Postings in Universal Journal

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: way to archive the company code after deactivation?
entry.content:
Hello @jerrysam ,

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (2SL):

You can deactivate and block a company code.
You cannot archive or delete the company code.
Historical data remains for legal and audit compliance.

 

Situation / QuestionExplanation, Resolution & SAP Notes
Company code has been deactivated in 2SL systemIn S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, organizational units like Company Code cannot be technically archived or deleted once productive postings exist. Deactivation only prevents further operational use.
Can a company code be archived?❌ No. Archiving object for company code (like ECC object FI_COMPANY) is not available in Public Cloud. Organizational units are protected for audit and data integrity reasons.
Why deletion/archiving is not allowed?Company code is a foundational org unit linked to financial postings (ACDOCA), tax reporting, asset accounting, etc. Removing it would break document integrity. 📌 SAP Note 2831001 – Restrictions on Changing/Deleting Organizational and Accounting Data in S/4HANA Cloud
What happens after deactivation?Deactivated company code: • Cannot be used for new postings • Remains available for reporting • Remains in historical documents
Is there a “Delete Company Code” SSCUI in Public Cloud?❌ No SSCUI exists to delete company codes in Public Cloud. Scope items delivered via CBC are not removable once active.
Can SAP Support remove it?❌ No. SAP does not remove company codes in productive tenants due to legal retention and audit compliance. 📌 Refer SAP Help: Organizational Units in SAP S/4HANA Cloud – Lifecycle Restrictions
What is the recommended approach?Best practice: ✔ Block for posting (all account types) ✔ Lock users/roles ✔ Close all open items ✔ Complete final financial statements ✔ Retain for reporting purposes
What about data retention & archiving?Transactional data (FI documents, assets, etc.) can be archived via Data Aging / ILM where supported, but the company code itself remains in the system. 📌 SAP Note 2248229 – Data Retention and ILM in S/4HANA
If created by mistake in early phaseIn 2SL before production go-live and before postings exist, SAP may allow correction via reconfiguration in CBC (Central Business Configuration). After productive use, removal is not supported.
Final AnswerArchiving or deleting a company code in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is not possible. Only deactivation and blocking for posting are supported.

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: Ensuring Auto-Submission of Timesheets Without Overti...
entry.content:
To ensure auto-submission of timesheets without including overtime hours, you need a proper time recording system. Set up your software to track only regular working hours and automatically submit them at the end of the day or week. This prevents mistakes and saves time for both employees and managers. Make sure the system clearly separates regular hours from overtime and allows easy review before submission.

Using a reliable tool like WorkDesQ can help manage attendance, working hours, and automatic timesheet submission efficiently, keeping everything accurate and organized.

entry.title: Re: RFM_ALLOCATION_STRATEGY BAdI in s4hana public cloud
entry.content:
Dear @KhaingZinTun,

RFM_ALLOCATION_STRATEGY will not appear in the Custom Logic app because SAP has not released it for Key User (in-app) extensibility. In Public Cloud there is no CBC/scope/configuration switch that can make a Not Released for Key User Extensibility Badi show up in Custom Logic.

Summary:

  • Key User Extensibility: Not Released so you cannot implement it via Custom Logic
  • Developer Extensibility: Released so you can implement it but only via ABAP Cloud / Developer Extensibility

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/64609d0ecac54654b0837cba34555b82/2099c7536e8e2a4be10000000a174cb4.html

 

https://api.sap.com/badi/RFM_ALLOCATION_STRATEGY

 

Additional information from SAP Note that can help in the future:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3558790

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: What is new in S/4HANA Manufacturing for Production Engineering & Operations (PEO) in 2025 FPS1
entry.content:
With the 2025 FPS1 release of SAP S/4HANA Manufacturing for Production Engineering and Operations (PEO), SAP continues to strengthen its manufacturing capabilities by delivering features that boost engineering efficiency, improve shop floor flexibility, and streamline daily operations.

In this blog, I’ll walk you through the key innovations included in this feature pack – and what they mean for production engineers, supervisors, and manufacturing operations teams.

 

Production Engineering Efficiency Enhancements

🔁Alternative Sequences of Operations in Shop Floor Routings

PEO now allows production engineers to create alternative and parallel sequences directly within shop floor routings.

This is especially helpful when:

  • Work can be done on an alternative machine
  • Different inspection paths exist
  • Certain steps can be bypassed
  • Different lot-size specific process flows are required

Supervisors can switch to these sequences at the order level, giving operations more flexibility without modifying the standard routing.

 

Why it matters:

Greater process and scheduling flexibility → reduced lead time, better resource utilization, and faster response to shop floor contingencies. 

🧩 Mass Management of Components in Order-Specific Shop Floor Routings

Engineers can now manage multiple components at once—adding, editing, or replacing them in a single table-based view.

Items marked Not Editable remain protected.

Why it matters:

Huge efficiency gains during order changes and rework scenarios, where batch updates to components are common. 

🔄Manage Alternative Components Using Alternative Item Groups (AIGs)

PEO now supports creating and managing alternative item groups for order-specific shop floor routings.

You can:

  • Add, edit, or remove alternative components
  • Move components into new/existing AIGs
  • Create new AIGs for new components

Why it matters:

Allows the shop floor to use valid alternatives without touching master data — a big win for flexibility and compliance. 

🔧Flexible Rework Routings

Engineers can now create rework routings with flexible processing, enabling:

  • Sequence-independent processing
  • Cross-operation predecessor relationships

This requires the production order’s routing to have flexible processing enabled as well.

Why it matters:

Rework becomes smoother and more adaptable, reducing bottlenecks and manual coordination. 

📝  Long Texts for Externally Processed Operations

Long text descriptions can now be authored externally processed operations — and the system automatically transfers this text to:

  • The production order
  • The purchase requisition

Why it matters:

Clearer communication with suppliers + fewer manual updates = reduced errors and faster processing. 

🎨Custom Text Formats via New BAdI

A new BAdI allows companies to define custom heading formats in the rich text editor used for standard texts and work instructions.

Why it matters:

Guarantees consistent formatting aligned with corporate branding standards, without manual adjustments by engineers. 

2. Flexible Production Operations Enhancements

🔀Order Split for Serialized Products with Sequences

PEO now supports splitting shop floor orders even when serialized products use alternative or parallel sequences.

PEO automatically determines which sequences remain feasible for the new order.

Why it matters:

Significant time savings by allowing completed units to progress independently, reducing waiting time and simplifying sequencing.

 

⏸️ Action Handlers for Hold/Release Operations

Production process specialists can now configure automated or manual actions that trigger when applying or releasing a hold—for example:

  • Additional quality checks
  • Automatic follow-on system actions

 

Why it matters:

More controlled, automated, and auditable hold handling with reduced manual overhead.

 

🛠️ Additional Code Groups for Defect Recording

Supervisors can now define additional reason code groups for defect recording. Operators can then choose from any of these groups.

 

Why it matters:

More accurate defect categorization → richer insights and more effective quality improvement initiatives.

 

Wrapping Up

The 2025 FPS1 updates for SAP S/4HANA PEO focus on one overarching theme:

Empowering production engineers and shop floor teams with flexibility, speed, and clarity.

Whether it’s expanding routing capabilities, streamlining rework, improving communication with suppliers, or making shop floor execution more adaptable, these features help manufacturers operate with greater agility and precision.

If you’re already using PEO, many of these enhancements can immediately simplify daily operations. If you’re planning a transition to S/4HANA Manufacturing, these innovations highlight why PEO continues to be a strong foundation for digital manufacturing.

entry.title: Message no. CK869 display immediately / Meldungsnr. CK869 sofort anzeigen
entry.content:
Message no. CK 869 (Material XXXXXX in plant XXXX, itemised does not match cost component split) only appears when a calculation (CK13N) is recalled. This means that incorrect values can be posted in the material price without anyone noticing. Is there a solution to prevent this?

Kind regards

 

Die Meldungsnr. CK 869 (Für Material XXXXXX im Werk XXXX weicht Einzelnachweis von Kostenschichtung ab) erscheint erst bei Wiederaufruf einer Kalkulation (CK13N) . Dadurch können falsche Werte im Materialpreis gebucht werden, ohne dass es auffällt. Gibt es eine Lösung, um dies zu verhindern

Viele Grüße

entry.title: Re: Event based Revenue Recognition for service dokuments...
entry.content:
Hello Manuel,

Technically it should work like this:

In customizing activity Maintain Settings for Event-Based Revenue Recognition

(view cluster FINS_TRR) you should check for the applied assignment rule for the revenue keys under consideration. Example:

 

 

Then you can check under sources which source the revenue account 40000009 is assigned to.

In the step Assign Sources and Posting Rules for this source set the value for the cost element to 40000009. Revenue adjustment postings by EBRR will then also be posted to P&L account 40000009. Example:

 

 

 

Look up some further explanations on EBRR customizing in https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/f63dd39a28bb4b90adbf9e608aff58ea/917c9fa4c78e49c7853261070570337e.html?version=2023.latest

Such a change should be made with care and thoroughly tested. For instance, the revenue account and the revenue adjustment account usually have different semantic tags assigned in the financial statement version. These tags are used as far as I know in several reporting and analysis tools, for instance in the EBRR Fiori app Event-Based Revenue Recognition – Service Documents. If billed revenue and revenue adjustment account are the same, the same semantic tag will be applied for both which can potentially have a bunch of expected consequences. At least I have not heard of anyone trying this.

Best Regards

Frank

entry.title: Re: not able to find the documents to download for tax cl...
entry.content:
Good day @jerrysam 

Thank you for your question.

The Doc No 2000004751 shown in your Excel is most likely the tax return document identifier, that was created potentially by the India Advance Return App, which is not a normal FI document number. That is why you cannot find it Display Journal Entry App.

You can view it by opening the same app and open monitor view and then search for the Doc No.

If it also created an accounting document, you could find it in Manage Journal Entries by searching in the Reference field with that Doc Number.

Below is a test on a European (not Indian specific) Advance Return app

Here I extract a list with a defined timeframe.

When I search in the system with the number 1800000000, I find it.

When I export the list, I can find the same number(s) without any other number. 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud Go-Live Success: A Comprehensive Checklist for Driving User Adoption
entry.content:
Implementing SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) successfully requires more than just technical deployment—it demands a strategic approach to ensure optimal performance, user adoption, and long-term value. This comprehensive guide outlines the critical areas organizations must address to achieve a successful SAC go-live for both Analytics and Planning scenarios.

The Foundation: Four Pillars of SAC Success

A successful SAC implementation rests on four fundamental pillars that must be carefully evaluated and optimized:

  1. Data Acquisition and Live Connection
  2. Modeling Excellence
  3. Story Design Optimization
  4. Ongoing House Keeping

Let's explore each pillar in detail to understand how they contribute to driving adoption and consumption in your SAC environment.

Pillar 1: Data Acquisition and Live Connection

The foundation of any analytics solution lies in robust data connectivity. For SAC implementations, this means ensuring your infrastructure can handle the demands of real-time analytics and planning scenarios.

Network and Infrastructure Considerations

Your network configuration becomes critical, especially when implementing live connections to source systems. This includes:

  • Device compatibility testing across laptops and mobile devices to ensure consistent user experience
  • Source system capacity evaluation for live scenarios to prevent performance bottlenecks
  • Tenant capacity planning, particularly crucial for import scenarios and planning workloads

End-to-End Connection Validation

A comprehensive review of your SAC connections should cover multiple technical layers:

  • Cloud Connector configuration and optimization
  • Authentication and Single Sign-On (SSO) setup
  • User authorization frameworks
  • Browser behavior testing in realistic business scenarios

These elements work together to create a seamless user experience that encourages adoption rather than creating barriers.

Pillar 2: Modeling Excellence

Effective modeling in SAC requires balancing functionality with performance, ensuring your solution serves actual business needs rather than simply showcasing available data.

Requirement Alignment and Simplification

Before diving into complex modeling scenarios, evaluate whether your requirements align with SAC's standard capabilities. Ask yourself:

  • Do your needs fit SAC's out-of-the-box functionality?
  • Are you creating unnecessary workarounds for complex workflows?
  • Is your commenting and large table handling approach sustainable?

Business-Focused Design Philosophy

The most successful SAC implementations follow a principle of designing for the business goal rather than building from all available data. This approach involves:

  • Keeping models simple and efficient by using only truly necessary data
  • Reducing data volume early in the modeling process
  • Avoiding unnecessary complexity that can hinder maintenance and scaling
  • Designing for maintainability to ensure long-term success

SAC Best Practices Implementation

Following established SAC best practices ensures your solution is secure, reliable, and provides smooth workflows. Key areas include:

  • Proper date handling and hierarchy design
  • Robust access control implementation
  • Comprehensive validation frameworks
  • Optimized planning area configuration

Pillar 3: Story Design Optimization

User experience in SAC stories directly impacts adoption rates. Poor performance or clunky interfaces can quickly derail even the most well-intentioned analytics initiatives.

Script and Event Optimization

Pay special attention to story pages utilizing scripts in critical events:

  • Review "onInitialization" and "onActive" event implementations
  • Eliminate unnecessary widget refreshes that can slow down user interactions
  • Optimize long-running data actions and multi-actions for better performance

Performance Testing and Validation

Comprehensive performance testing should cover both individual and concurrent usage scenarios:

  • Single-user performance optimization to ensure responsive individual experiences
  • High user concurrency testing to validate system behavior under realistic load conditions
  • Realistic scenario and volume testing to identify potential bottlenecks before go-live

Pillar 4: House Keeping and Ongoing Maintenance

Successful SAC implementations require ongoing attention to system health and optimization opportunities.

System Monitoring and Health Checks

Regular monitoring of your SAC environment should include:

  • Homepage System Overview reviews to identify potential issues early
  • Import job failure monitoring to ensure data freshness and reliability
  • Data action performance tracking to identify optimization opportunities
  • Long-running query analysis to prevent user experience degradation

Job Optimization and Scheduling

Efficient job management contributes significantly to overall system performance:

  • Review calendar job cadence regularly
  • Adjust or reduce job frequency where possible without impacting business requirements
  • Balance system resource utilization across different time periods

Driving Adoption Through Excellence

The ultimate goal of following this comprehensive checklist is to create an SAC environment that users want to engage with regularly. When data connections are reliable, models are intuitive, stories perform well, and the system runs smoothly, user adoption naturally follows.

Organizations that systematically address each of these four pillars typically see higher user engagement, better business outcomes, and stronger return on their SAC investment. The key is treating go-live not as a destination, but as the beginning of an ongoing optimization journey.

Next Steps for Your SAC Implementation

As you prepare for your SAC go-live or optimize an existing implementation, consider conducting a comprehensive review across all four pillars. Start with the areas that most directly impact your users' daily experience, then work systematically through the technical infrastructure elements that support those experiences. If you seek expert services and guidance for any of the areas please check our engagement plans and service portfolio.

Remember, successful SAC adoption is built on the foundation of technical excellence, user-centric design, and ongoing operational discipline. By following this checklist and maintaining focus on these core principles, your organization can maximize the value of its SAC investment while driving meaningful business outcomes through enhanced analytics and planning capabilities.

entry.title: ABAP Cloud Learning Map
entry.content:
The ABAP Cloud development model (ABAP Cloud) is the foundation of ABAP-based development at SAP, its partners, and customers, designed from the ground up to adhere to clean core principles. It brings together evolving technologies and methodologies into a sustainable, long-lasting programming model. 

The ABAP Cloud development model encompasses all aspects of modern cloud-ready software development, resulting in a wide range of topics that need to be understood. Therefore, clear guidance is helpful for getting started with and navigating ABAP Cloud.

Discover the ABAP Cloud Learning Map🗺️ – your guide to mastering the ABAP Cloud development model. It provides an overview of essential topics and skills, along with a curated collection of links to key resources covering the technologies, tools, approaches, and practices that drive cloud-ready, upgrade-stable ABAP development. 📚Whether you’re starting out or filling knowledge gaps, the learning map helps you navigate modern ABAP Cloud development at your own pace. 

🔗Access it here: ABAP Cloud Learning Map↗

entry.title: Re: Issue with AI160 JavaScript Exercise – /api/agent/tri...
entry.content:
This question was resolved in GitHub issue #26.

The problem was a port conflict with SAP Business Application Studio (3000/3001). The application already occupies those ports, so the UI proxy hits an HTML error page instead of the agent route.

The latest version of the demo on GitHub improves the error messaging to make port conflicts more obvious.

To resolve the conflict, update the ports used by the mock server and agent server.

  1. Update the exercises/javascript/app/mock-server/src/server.ts file to set the mock server port to 3005 (or another free port).
// exercises/javascript/app/mock-server/src/server.ts
-const port = process.env.PORT || 3001;
+const port = process.env.PORT || 3005;
  1. Update the exercises/javascript/app/agent/src/setup.ts file to point the base URL to port 3005.
// exercises/javascript/app/agent/src/setup.ts
export const BASE_URL_API_PURCHASEORDER_PROCESS_SRV =
`http://localhost:3005/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_PURCHASEORDER_PROCESS_SRV/`;
  1. Update the exercises/javascript/app/agent/src/server.ts file to set the agent server port to 3006 (or another free port).
// exercises/javascript/app/agent/src/server.ts
-const port = process.env.PORT || 3001;
+const port = process.env.PORT || 3006;
  1. Update the exercises/javascript/app/ui/vite.config.ts file to point the UI proxy to port 3006.
// exercises/javascript/app/ui/vite.config.ts
- target: 'http://localhost:3001',
+ target: 'http://localhost:3006',

After these changes, the preview endpoint should route correctly, and the agent call should stop returning “HTML/404”.

entry.title: Re: Can I resell Crystal Reports purchased from the SAP S...
entry.content: Dear customer, no you cannot purchase on SAP Store and resell to another company. The buyer needs to be the user and the payer. SAP Store is direct sales only. For OEM agreements please contact the SAP Partner team. Kind regards. Dominik

entry.title: Staging in SAP Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) – An Introduction to "Config Push"
entry.content:
Introduction

Motivation and goal

In software development, it is standard procedure to use staging for developed content, running it through quality assurance (QAS) before pushing it to the production system (PROD). This avoids the risk of developing directly in the production environment.

This key functionality has been missing in the Advanced Event Mesh (AEM), leading to the problem that developing directly in the production system is dangerous, and manually copying every event from one AEM to another is tedious.

This problem is solved with the new "Config Push" functionality, which is the main topic of this blog post. 
In the next few minutes, you will get the long-awaited answer to the question:

"How can you effectively implement staging in the AEM?"

This blog seeks to enable you to fully utilize this new functionality and profit from a more robust and less tedious AEM architectural landscape that now natively supports staging.

Covered content

In the first chapter, a brief overview of the AEM and the Event Portal will be given to allow everyone to fully understand the new functionality. If you're already familiar with the AEM and the Event Portal, you can skip ahead to the second chapter, where a step-by-step tutorial on how to enable and use the functionality is provided. If you have limited time or just want to get a quick glimpse of the new functionality, you can also jump ahead to the last chapter, where the most important aspects and steps will be summarized.


Brief overview of the AEM and the Event Portal

AEM supports many key features that come along with the broker and the entitlement like the Event Portal, Micro-Integrations, Mission Control and Insights.
Overview of functionality
The key feature this blog focuses on is the Event Portal, a tool used for designing your events. To use it effectively, you should first consider your business processes that you have and then build events based on them. This design-first approach is developer friendly, as it allows you to create and refine all your events before pushing them to the system.
 
Designed Runtime in the Event Portal Designer
The Event Portal Designer also gives you a full holistic view of the ecosystem. Within the components view all the consumed applications and events are displayed and you are able to directly add subscribers.
Component view
It enables you to see which which applications publish and subscribe an event.
Event details
You can also view the details from an application's perspective.
Application Details
Now the question is, once you designed the runtime, how can you have a lifecycle version control in place and push all the created controls to the broker?
This question is answered by the new functionality "Config Push".
 

How to enable staging by using "Config Push"

Once you have a designed Application Domain, you can now push it to the running broker. 
All the available AEM brokers can be found under the "Cluster Manager" tab.
AEM Brokers in the cluster manager

 

Push applications to DEV broker

  1. First you have to have created a runtime and connect it to the broker
    1. Go to Runtime Manager
      1. Runtime manager 
    2. Click on "Create Modeled Event Mesh" at the top right
      1. Create modeled event mesh modal
    3. Click on the new Modeled Event Mesh 
      1. Open modeled Event Mesh
    4. Go to the "Event Broker Connections Tab"
      1. Event broker connections tab
    5. Click on "Connect Event Broker" and select the broker you want to connect to
      1. Connect event broker
    6. It should now look like this. Every push that will be done is now against this broker
      1. Event Broker Connections
    7. Under "Architecture" all the objects that will be pushed are displayed
      1. Architecture tab
  2. Next you have to add the Applications to the runtime.
    1. Go again into the Designer and click on your Application Domain
      1. Application Domain
    2. Click on the Application that you want to push and open the application details (can also be done via the components view)
    3. Add the runtime and broker in the configuration of the application 
      1. Configuration
      2. Select the broker that is also connected to the runtime
        1. Select broker
      3. Define the client credentials
        1. Set client credentials
        2. Client Credentials can be found in the Cluster Manager > Broker > Try Me
          1. Find client credentials
      4. Click "Save & Promote" Later
        1. Save and promote later
    4. Promote Application
      1. Go to "Promotions", click on "Promote Version" and select the broker
      2. Click on "Preview Promotion"
        1.  
      3. Click on "Promote"
    5. The pushed application should now have a checkmark. Now check that the application was deployed by navigating to the previously created runtime under Runtime Manager > Runtime > Architecture. The deployed application should now be displayed
  3. Congratulations. You now have pushed your first application to the dev environment. You can now redo steps 2.1 to 2.4 for the other applications that you want to push.

 

Push applications to the other environment brokers

To push your applications to other subaccounts, your landscape must contain multiple subaccounts with the AEM that have a one to one connection. One subaccount for development (DEV), one subaccount for quality assurance (QAS) and one subaccount for production (PROD). Note that this blog only demonstrates linking to the PROD environment.
 
With the "Config Push" feature, you can push your previously promoted applications to the other environments, such as QAS or PROD, directly from the DEV AEM Event Portal. This is possible even though the subaccounts are normally isolated and unaware of one another.
  1. First you have to set up the subaccount landscape by linking the secondary AEM subaccounts to the main DEV subaccount 
    1. PROD: Go to your PROD Subaccount and into Account Details > Account Settings
      1. PROD: Enable linking
      2. (DEV: When trying to enable it in the DEV subaccount it is disabled, as in the subaccount that is linked to another subaccount no designing should be taking place. All the designing should be done in the DEV subaccount. Once you enable the "Subaccount Linking" the Event Portal will be disabled)
        1.  DEV: Enable Linking
    2. PROD: Click on "Create Link Code"
      1. PROD: Create Link Code
    3. PROD: Click on "Copy"
      1. PROD: Copy
      2. DEV: Paste link code in the Event Portal subaccount
        1. DEV: Go to DEV subaccount AEM Environments Page
          1. DEV: Environment Page
        2. DEV: Create new Environment
          1. DEV: Create environment
        3. DEV: Select "For brokers in a secondary AEM subaccount
          1. DEV: select for secondary
        4. DEV: Name it accordingly and select "Production" if its the Production subaccount
          1. DEV: Select production
        5. DEV: Click on "Link Now", paste the "Link Code" that you copied previously and click on "Link"
          1. DEV: Paste and link
        6. DEV: Now you can create a "Modeled Event Mesh" by navigating to the Runtime Event Manager for the subaccount
          1. DEV: Create Modeled Event Mesh
        7. DEV: After that you only have to click on the Modeled Event Mesh, go to "Event Broker Connections", click on "Connect Event Broker" and connect the production broker
          1. DEV: Connect Event Broker
  2. Now you have set up the subaccount landscape, you can push your promoted applications and events to other stages!
 

Summary 

This blog introduced "Config Push," a new capability for the AEM designed to streamline configuration management across multiple environments. It provides a comprehensive overview of how to leverage the Event Portal to design and promote application configurations. The result is a more robust workflow for managing the AEM landscape.
 
The core process involves these four steps:
  1. Establish the Development Environment: Create a "Modeled Event Mesh" within the Runtime Manager and connect it to the designated development broker.
  2. Deploy to Development: Configure the target application within the Event Portal Designer, associate it with the development runtime, and use the "Promote" function to deploy it.
  3. Link Subsequent Environments: Generate a "Link Code" from a secondary subaccount (for example Production) and use it within the primary development subaccount to establish a trusted connection between the environments.
  4. Promote to Production: Once linked, create a new Modeled Event Mesh for the production stage, connect the corresponding broker, and promote the validated application configurations to the live environment.

I hope this introduction to "Config Push" has provided you some valuable and useful insights. 

Feel free to comment any questions and remarks!

entry.title: Re: SAP IoT Edge to SAP CPI Integration – Enterprise Architecture Explained

Hi Shiva, sorry to put it so bluntly but SAP IoT and SAP Edge are not offered anymore since 2022.

Different SAP applications have replaced it with different, use cases specific technologies. In my area, SAP Asset Performance Management, we have replaced the technical component with Cumulocity which enables some of the scenarios you describe in the context of Asset Maintenance. I suggest you refer to the supply chain blogs like this one https://community.sap.com/t5/supply-chain-management-blog-posts-by-sap/embedded-iot-is-here-5-apps-that-accelerate-sensor-to-insight-in-sap-asset/ba-p/14265769 to learn more.
