entry.title: Two SDI Errors That Will Ruin Your Friday: Cleanup Environment Prohibited and Persistent Data Store
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE  |  SAP SMART DATA INTEGRATION

Two SDI Errors That Will Ruin Your Friday (And How to Fix Both)

A field guide to debugging "Cleanup Environment Prohibited" and "Persistent Data Store Full" in SAP HANA Smart Data Integration — with the exact SQL that solves both.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP Architect  |  HANA Data Engineering  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Two of the most disruptive SDI errors in production aren't caused by bad code — they're caused by state that accumulates silently until it blocks everything. This post covers both: how to diagnose them, the exact SQL to run, and the manual cleanup steps that actually work.

The Setup

SAP Smart Data Integration (SDI) is one of the most powerful data movement capabilities in the HANA ecosystem. It's also one of the least forgiving when it comes to operational hygiene. Unlike a failed SQL query that you fix and re-run, SDI errors tend to be stateful — they reflect accumulated conditions in the replication engine that don't resolve themselves and don't always surface obvious diagnostic paths.

This article covers two errors that I've encountered in production SDI environments and that I see regularly in community questions without complete answers: the Cleanup Environment Prohibited error and the Persistent Data Store Full error. Both are solvable. Neither is well documented end-to-end. Here's exactly what to do.

ERROR 1 OF 2 

Cleanup Environment Prohibited

What You See

SDI Error: Cleanup of the replication environment is currently prohibited.

This error surfaces when you attempt to drop, reset, or clean up a remote subscription — or when a failed replication task leaves the SDI agent and HANA in an inconsistent state. The engine is telling you it cannot safely clean up because it believes there is active state it would be destroying.

The frustrating reality is that the 'active state' it's protecting is often already broken. Subscriptions that failed mid-replication, orphaned triggers on the source system, and remote subscription metadata that no longer corresponds to a real replication task can all cause this. HANA won't let you clean up until you prove the state is safe to remove — and doing that requires manual interrogation.

Step 1 — Understand What You're Looking At

Before running any cleanup statements, establish a clear picture of the current state. Start with your column counts to identify which tables have problems:

-- Check column counts to identify tables with anomalies

-- Tables with unexpectedly low column counts often indicate

-- incomplete replication or schema mismatch after a failed load

SELECT COUNT(column_name), table_name

FROM table_columns

WHERE schema_name LIKE '<your_schema_name>'

GROUP BY table_name

ORDER BY COUNT(column_name) ASC;

Tables appearing at the top of this result (lowest column counts) are your first area of investigation. A table that should have 80 columns showing 3 is a sign that the subscription was interrupted during initial load and the schema was only partially applied.

Next, check the current state of all remote subscriptions in the affected schema:

-- View all remote subscriptions — check STATUS and ERROR_MESSAGE columns

SELECT *

FROM M_REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTIONS

WHERE SCHEMA_NAME LIKE '<your_schema_name>'

ORDER BY SUBSCRIPTION_NAME ASC;

Pay attention to the STATUS column. You are looking for subscriptions in ERROR, INITIALIZING, or SUSPENDED states — these are the ones blocking cleanup. A subscription in ACTIVE state with a healthy last_processed timestamp is not your problem. Everything else is a candidate for reset.

Cross-reference with record counts to confirm which tables are genuinely populated vs. which are empty shells from failed initializations:

-- Compare record counts — run in both source and target to identify gaps

SELECT table_name, record_count

FROM m_tables

WHERE schema_name LIKE '<your_schema_name>'

ORDER BY TABLE_NAME ASC;

Step 2 — Generate and Run the RESET Statements

Once you know which subscriptions need to be reset, generate the ALTER REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION RESET statements. Running these manually for large schemas is error-prone — generate them programmatically:

-- Generate ALTER REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION RESET for all affected subscriptions

-- Copy output and execute in sequence

SELECT

  'ALTER REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION '

  || SCHEMA_NAME || '.' || SUBSCRIPTION_NAME

  || ' RESET;'

FROM M_REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTIONS

WHERE SCHEMA_NAME LIKE '<your_schema_name>';

Execute the generated statements. The format for each individual reset is:

ALTER REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION "<schema_name>"."<subscription_name>" RESET;

Important: RESET moves the subscription back to a state where HANA believes it is safe to clean up. It does not drop the subscription or delete data. Think of it as telling the engine: this subscription is no longer in active use, you can safely proceed with cleanup operations.

Step 3 — Clean Up the Source System

This is the step most documentation skips entirely. The Cleanup Environment Prohibited error often persists even after running RESET statements because orphaned objects remain on the source system. SDI creates triggers and shadow tables on the source to capture change data — if these aren't removed, the engine sees residual ownership and refuses full cleanup.

Run the following on the source system to identify what SDI left behind:

-- Find all objects owned by the SDI replication user

SELECT * FROM SYS.OWNERSHIP

WHERE OWNER_NAME LIKE '<sdi_user_name>%'

ORDER BY OWNER_NAME DESC;

-- Generate DROP statements for SDI-created triggers

SELECT 'DROP TRIGGER ' || SCHEMA_NAME || '.' || TRIGGER_NAME || ';'

FROM TRIGGERS

WHERE OWNER_NAME LIKE '<sdi_user_name>%';

For the shadow tables (HADP% tables) that SDI creates in the source schema, generate DROP statements with CASCADE to handle any dependent objects:

-- Generate DROP statements for SDI shadow tables

-- CASCADE handles dependent views or synonyms

SELECT

  'CONNECT ' || SCHEMA_NAME || ' PASSWORD <pwd>;'

  || CHR(10) ||

  'DROP TABLE ' || SCHEMA_NAME || '.' || TABLE_NAME || ' CASCADE;'

FROM TABLES

WHERE SCHEMA_NAME LIKE '<source_schema>%'

AND TABLE_NAME LIKE 'HADP%';

Verify the shadow tables are actually present before running drops — confirm with a SELECT first:

-- Verify HADP shadow tables exist before dropping

SELECT * FROM TABLES

WHERE SCHEMA_NAME LIKE '<source_schema>%'

AND TABLE_NAME LIKE 'HADP%';

-- Also check for HADP-related triggers that may remain

SELECT * FROM TRIGGERS

WHERE TRIGGER_NAME LIKE '%HADP%'

AND TRIGGER_NAME LIKE '%LAST%'

ORDER BY SCHEMA_NAME DESC;

After completing source system cleanup, return to HANA and verify M_REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTIONS shows no remaining subscriptions for the affected schema in ERROR or SUSPENDED state. If residual subscriptions remain, re-run the RESET generation query and execute again.

ERROR 2 OF 2 

Persistent Data Store Full

What You See

SDI Error: The persistent data store used by the Data Provisioning Agent is full.

This error is more operationally straightforward than the first, but it stops replication completely and tends to arrive without much warning. The persistent data store is a local storage area maintained by the SDI Data Provisioning Agent to buffer change data captured from source systems before it's applied to HANA. When this store fills up, the agent can no longer queue new changes and replication halts.

The common causes are a burst of change data volume that exceeds the normal buffer capacity, a replication lag that allowed the queue to grow unchecked, and — most commonly in my experience — subscriptions that stopped consuming from the data store while the source kept writing to it.

Step 1 — Diagnose Which Remote Source Is the Culprit

Before taking any action, identify exactly which remote source is consuming the most persistent data store space. This single query gives you the complete picture:

-- Check persistent data store usage by remote source

-- SIZE is in bytes — divide by 1073741824 for GB

SELECT

  REMOTE_SOURCE_NAME,

  SUM(SIZE) AS TOTAL_SIZE_BYTES,

  ROUND(SUM(SIZE) / 1073741824, 2) AS TOTAL_SIZE_GB

FROM REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTION_DATA_CONTAINERS

GROUP BY REMOTE_SOURCE_NAME

ORDER BY TOTAL_SIZE_BYTES DESC;

The remote source at the top of this result is your primary offender. In most cases you will find one source consuming a disproportionate share — this is almost always a subscription that has stalled while the source continues generating change data.

Drill into the specific containers for that source to understand the composition:

-- Get container-level detail for the problematic remote source

SELECT *

FROM REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTION_DATA_CONTAINERS

WHERE REMOTE_SOURCE_NAME = '<your_remote_source_name>'

ORDER BY SIZE DESC;

Cross-reference the container names against M_REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTIONS to identify which subscriptions are associated with the largest containers and what their current replication status is.

Step 2 — Resolution Path

The resolution depends on what you found in Step 1. There are two scenarios:

Scenario A — The subscription is still valid and you want to keep it running. The data store is full because replication has lagged, not because the subscription is broken. In this case, the priority is to allow the agent to catch up by temporarily reducing the change data capture rate or pausing other subscriptions sharing the same agent, then monitoring the store size as the backlog clears.

Scenario B — The subscription is broken, stale, or no longer needed. This is the more common production scenario. Run the RESET and cleanup sequence from Error 1 for the affected subscriptions, which will release the data store containers associated with them. After the subscriptions are reset and dropped, the persistent data store space is reclaimed by the agent.

Monitoring recommendation: After resolving the immediate issue, set up a recurring alert on REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTION_DATA_CONTAINERS size. A threshold alert at 70% capacity gives you enough runway to investigate before replication halts. The query from Step 1 is exactly what you need for a monitoring job — schedule it and route results to your operations team.

Quick Reference: Side-by-Side Comparison

 

Cleanup Environment Prohibited

Persistent Data Store Full

Root cause

Orphaned subscription state + source system residue

SDI agent buffer filled by lagging or stalled subscription

First query to run

M_REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTIONS — check STATUS column

REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTION_DATA_CONTAINERS — check SIZE by source

Primary fix

ALTER REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION RESET + source cleanup

Reset stale subscriptions OR let active subscription catch up

Source system action needed?

Yes — drop triggers and HADP shadow tables

No — HANA-side resolution only in most cases

Recurrence prevention

Implement subscription state monitoring job

Alert at 70% persistent data store capacity

 

What Both Errors Have in Common

The pattern underlying both of these errors is the same: SDI maintains state that HANA itself cannot automatically reconcile. Unlike a failed query that leaves no trace, a failed replication task leaves orphaned objects, stalled containers, and subscription metadata that persists until you manually clear it. The engine's caution is appropriate — it won't clean up state it isn't certain is safe to remove. Your job as the architect is to prove that safety through the diagnostic sequence above.

The most important operational takeaway is that both errors are easier to prevent than to fix. A scheduled monitoring job that checks M_REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTIONS for non-ACTIVE statuses and REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTION_DATA_CONTAINERS for size thresholds will catch both conditions before they become production incidents. The queries in this article are exactly what you need for that monitoring layer.

If you're hitting either of these errors and the steps above aren't resolving them, the most useful thing to include when asking for help is the full output of M_REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTIONS for your schema and the REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTION_DATA_CONTAINERS query result. Those two outputs contain almost everything needed to diagnose the specific variant you're dealing with.

entry.title: Re: Sneak Peek in to SAP Analytics Cloud release for Q1 2026
entry.content:
Hello @Orla_C ,

My question is about Story versioning. I have tried to use it, and it works well for one file. However, all versions disappears when copying the story. When such a story is "Save as" then versions are lost as well.

In our use case, developers copy a productive story in a separate folder, they change it there, and publish it back when the story is ready. In this scenario, versions disappear at the moment of copying or saving as.

If there is a way to use versions in this scenario, please let me know.

Br, Oleksandr
